item.title: One of the scenes that made “Wake Up Dead Man” my favorite part of the Knives Ou...
item.description:
@cinematologist_

One of the scenes that made “Wake Up Dead Man” my favorite part of the Knives Out series. In the middle of all that investigative chaos, a moment like this hit so powerfully. #wakeupdeadman #knivesout #joshoconnor @riancjohnson
item.content:encoded:
@cinematologist_

One of the scenes that made “Wake Up Dead Man” my favorite part of the Knives Out series. In the middle of all that investigative chaos, a moment like this hit so powerfully. #wakeupdeadman #knivesout #joshoconnor @riancjohnson
item.author: cinematologist_

item.title: Ever since I saw some of the stills used to present Rose, I’ve been counting dow...
item.description:
@cinematologist_

Ever since I saw some of the stills used to present Rose, I’ve been counting down the days until I could finally watch it. I was instantly intrigued: who is this person, when and where is the story set, is it based on real events, and what story lies behind her face? I got my answers at @diagonale_at where Rose opened this year’s edition. Its director, Markus Schleinzer, has worked as a casting director on over 60 projects, with credits including Amour, The White Ribbon (Michael Haneke), and Dog Days (Ulrich Seidl).
Rose is set in 17th-century Germany during the Thirty Years’ War and follows a woman who disguises herself as a man, taking on the identity of a dead soldier in order to inherit land, something that was only possible for men at the time. I couldn’t have been more grateful to see it in a venue usually used for concerts because the sound was insane, creating moments I could never experience at home. At times, the film echoes Béla Tarr, especially The Turin Horse, with glimpses of Carl Theodor Dreyer, but the film still carries a distinct voice of its own, elevated by its unexpected yet welcome touches of humor.
It was captivating to watch Sandra Hüller in a role where she appears confident and stoic on the surface, yet every interaction feels fragile, charged with tension, because only you and her share the weight of the secret.
Something that caught my eye more than once is how the film lets actors pause, breathe, and think saying so much with so little dialogue. Every line feels precise and singular. Gerald Kerkletz’s black and white cinematography is stunning, particularly in rain and snow. I love that the film was shot across different seasons. The prosthetic makeup is so expertly done that you quickly accept it as her face, not something she’s wearing. The narration is beautiful, though I think it wasn’t really needed to such an extent. Still, it’s not something I’d consider a drawback.
After the film ended, I walked home in silence, thinking about how disheartening it is that so many people today respond to gender oppression with the same herd mentality depicted in the film, set in the 17th century. #rose #sandrahüller @markusschleinzer
item.content:encoded:
@cinematologist_

Ever since I saw some of the stills used to present Rose, I’ve been counting down the days until I could finally watch it. I was instantly intrigued: who is this person, when and where is the story set, is it based on real events, and what story lies behind her face? I got my answers at @diagonale_at where Rose opened this year’s edition. Its director, Markus Schleinzer, has worked as a casting director on over 60 projects, with credits including Amour, The White Ribbon (Michael Haneke), and Dog Days (Ulrich Seidl).
Rose is set in 17th-century Germany during the Thirty Years’ War and follows a woman who disguises herself as a man, taking on the identity of a dead soldier in order to inherit land, something that was only possible for men at the time. I couldn’t have been more grateful to see it in a venue usually used for concerts because the sound was insane, creating moments I could never experience at home. At times, the film echoes Béla Tarr, especially The Turin Horse, with glimpses of Carl Theodor Dreyer, but the film still carries a distinct voice of its own, elevated by its unexpected yet welcome touches of humor.
It was captivating to watch Sandra Hüller in a role where she appears confident and stoic on the surface, yet every interaction feels fragile, charged with tension, because only you and her share the weight of the secret.
Something that caught my eye more than once is how the film lets actors pause, breathe, and think saying so much with so little dialogue. Every line feels precise and singular. Gerald Kerkletz’s black and white cinematography is stunning, particularly in rain and snow. I love that the film was shot across different seasons. The prosthetic makeup is so expertly done that you quickly accept it as her face, not something she’s wearing. The narration is beautiful, though I think it wasn’t really needed to such an extent. Still, it’s not something I’d consider a drawback.
After the film ended, I walked home in silence, thinking about how disheartening it is that so many people today respond to gender oppression with the same herd mentality depicted in the film, set in the 17th century. #rose #sandrahüller @markusschleinzer
item.author: cinematologist_

item.title: A scene from Jim Jarmusch’s 1989 film “Mystery Train”. #jimjarmusch
item.description:
@cinematologist_

A scene from Jim Jarmusch’s 1989 film “Mystery Train”. #jimjarmusch

@cinematologist_

A scene from Jim Jarmusch’s 1989 film “Mystery Train”. #jimjarmusch
